flags: No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted, enumerating every cut-list row.

CONTRADICTION IN THE SOURCE: the cut list gives the crown pieces (2 sides at 13", 1 front at 27") as 1x2 stock, while the materials list and the crown step both call for 'Cove moulding, 3/4" or smaller'. The prose describes fitting and mitering the crown without asserting which stock it comes from. Needs an author decision.

The exact arrangement of the six legs is not stated. The rewrite uses the only reading on which the 25-1/2" header top closes: two 1x3 on the front corners and one 1x3 plus one 1x2 down each side, adding 3/4" per side to the 24" case. Flagged as derived.
Shelf spacing for the four upper shelves is not authored — the rewrite tells the builder to choose and to mark both sides together, rather than inventing a number.
Front bottom trim at 20-1/2" does not reconcile with 24" case width minus two 2-1/2" legs (19"). Written as measure-to-fit, which the cut list itself notes.

step.title: Cut the case and leg parts
step.body: Every case part keeps the board's full width, so only the lengths change. From the three 8 ft 1x12s cut two sides 68" long, five bottom-and-shelf pieces 22-1/2", and one top 24"; those eight pieces pack into exactly three boards allowing 1/8" for each saw cut, so cut the two 68" sides first and take the shelves from what is left. From the four 8 ft 1x3s cut four legs at 68-3/4" — one per board — then take the two 11-1/4" header sides and the 25-1/2" header top from the offcuts. From the 1x2s cut two side legs at 68-3/4" and hold the rest of that stock back, since the side trim, front bottom trim and crown are all measured off the assembled piece rather than cut to a number. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

ops.body: Stand the two 68" sides on edge and set the five 22-1/2" shelves between them, so the case comes to 24" wide overall — 22-1/2" plus 3/4" of side stock at each end. The 24" top then caps over both sides flush, and the optional back panel at 24" x 68-3/4" confirms that reading. Put the bottom shelf flush with the bottom ends of the sides, then space the remaining four wherever you want your books to sit, marking both sides together so every shelf lands level. Glue each joint and drive 2" brad nails through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends, nailing the top on last. Measure both diagonals across the open front before the glue sets — on a case 68-3/4" tall and only 24" wide a small rack shows badly.

The header is the band that wraps the top of the case just under the crown, and it is fitted to your own piece rather than to a number. The two header sides are the case depth, 11-1/4" as drawn, and the header top spans the full trimmed front, which comes to 25-1/2" once the 3/4" side legs are on. Hold each piece against the case and mark it in place instead of measuring twice. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the case sides and top, keeping the header's top edge flush with the top of the case so the crown has a flat landing.

The legs are what give this piece its tower proportions, and all six are 68-3/4" — the full case height including the 3/4" top. Glue and nail a 1x3 to each front corner so its 2-1/2" face covers the case's front edge, then run a 1x3 and a 1x2 down each side so the applied stock adds 3/4" per side and brings the outside width to 25-1/2", which is exactly why the header top is cut 25-1/2". Keep the top ends of all six legs flush with the top of the case and start from the top, letting any length error fall at the floor where it will not show. Use 1-1/4" brads into the 3/4" case sides so nothing punches through the inside.

The two 7-1/4" 1x2 pieces fill the short gaps left on the sides between the applied legs. Measure the actual opening on your own piece and cut to that — the 7-1/4" in the cut list assumes the legs landed exactly where drawn, and yours will be a little different. Cut each a whisker long and pare it back until it presses in without forcing. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the outside face flush with the legs on either side so the whole side reads as one plane.

The front bottom trim closes the front of the case at the floor, between the two front legs. Measure the actual gap between your legs and cut to that number; the 20-1/2" in the cut list is a starting value and the cut list itself marks it measure-and-cut-to-fit. Hold it with its bottom edge flush with the bottom of the case and its face flush with the legs, then glue it and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the front edge of the bottom shelf.

The back is optional, and skipping it is a fair choice if the tower will always stand against a wall. If you fit one, cut it 24" wide by 68-3/4" tall from the 1/4" plywood or MDF — that is the full outside footprint of the case including the top, so it covers the back edges rather than sitting inside them. Square the case first, then use the panel to hold it square: line up one corner, tack it, check the diagonal, and only then nail the rest off with 3/4" brads and glue every 6" to 8".

Fit the crown last, after everything else is square and trimmed. Cut the two side returns first, holding each against the piece to mark its length, then fit the front piece between them — a front piece cut slightly long and shaved back closes tighter than one cut to a number. Miter the outside corners at 45° and dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue goes on. Keep the profile modest so it reads as a finished cap rather than an ornament, then glue the miters and pin with 3/4" brads into the header below.

Fill every nail hole with wood filler and let it dry fully before sanding, or the filler will smear rather than cut. Sand the whole piece at 120 grit to level the filler and the joints, then again at 180 to 220 before finishing, paying particular attention to the mitered crown corners and the leg edges — those are the details people actually look at on a piece like this. Prime and paint, or stain. A tower 68-3/4" tall and only 11-1/4" deep is easy to tip, so anchor it to a wall stud before it gets loaded with books.

baseStepTitles: Add the top and bottom side trim

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (arithmetic on the plan's own cut list): the 1x12 parts total 421" — three at 75-1/4", four at 25-1/2", four at 7-1/2", one at 36-1/4", one at 27" — while the declared 1x12 stock is three 8 ft boards plus one 10 ft, i.e. 408". Short by roughly 13" before any kerf. The cut step says to buy one more 8 ft 1x12.

Steps 6 and 7 (top and bottom trim, and the crown) have NO cut-list rows and no dimensions anywhere in the plan. Written as measure-and-cut-to-fit; the 1x2 stock does have surplus for it, but the sizes are not authored.
No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted.

The 27" back panel and the 27" face-frame rails together fix the cabinet at 27" wide overall; the face frame reading (rails run the full 27" and the 73-3/4" stiles sit between them) is arithmetic — 73-3/4" plus two 3/4" rails equals the 75-1/4" side length — and is stated with that proof.
The drawer-box reading is taken verbatim from run1-box-geometry.mjs: drawer sides run full length and fronts/backs sit between them.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Cut the 1x12s first, because the layout is tight: two sides and one main divider at 75-1/4", four top-and-bottom pieces at 25-1/2", one fixed shelf at 36-1/4", one bottom shelf at 27", and four cubby dividers at 7-1/2". Take each 75-1/4" piece off its own board. Be aware before you start that those parts total 421" of 1x12 while the declared lumber — three 8 ft boards and one 10 ft — comes to 408", so buy one more 8 ft 1x12 rather than discovering the shortfall at the last shelf. From the 1x2s cut two frame stiles at 73-3/4", two rails at 27" and a centre rail at 24". From the single 1x6 cut six drawer sides at 10-1/2" and three drawer fronts and backs at 5-3/4", treating both as starting sizes only. Hold the cubby dividers until you have measured your actual 1x8 — their 7-1/2" is your board's real width plus the 1/4" drawer allowance, and the 7-1/4" drawer face in the cut list is that same board width.

ops.body: The main divider is a full-height 75-1/4" 1x12 that splits the cabinet into the tall locker bay on one side and the stack of drawer cubbies on the other. Mark its position on both the top and the bottom panel before you fasten anything — the top and bottom are 25-1/2" and the divider takes 3/4" out of that, so the two bays share what is left, and a divider that leans throws every cubby opening out of square. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, or with countersunk screws or finish nails and glue if you skipped the jig. Check it square to the bottom panel in both directions before the glue grabs.

Add the two 75-1/4" sides and the 25-1/2" top and bottom panels around the divider, working on a flat floor. The cabinet finishes 27" wide overall, which is what both the 27" back panel and the 27" face-frame rails confirm. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, or countersunk screws or finish nails and glue — the face frame added later reinforces every one of these joints, so they do not have to carry the cabinet on their own. Drop the 27" bottom shelf and the 36-1/4" fixed shelf into their marked positions while the box is still open and easy to reach. Measure both diagonals of the front opening one last time and adjust until they match, because everything about the drawers depends on it.

Cut the 1/4" plywood back 27" by 76" — the cabinet's full outside footprint — and mark the position of every fixed shelf on the outside of it first, so your nails land in wood rather than in air. Glue and nail it to the sides, top, bottom and each fixed shelf with 1-1/4" finish nails. Tack one corner, confirm the diagonals still match, then nail the rest off at roughly 6" spacing. On a cabinet this tall the back is the only thing resisting racking, so do not leave it off.

Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the cabinet. The two 27" rails run the full width and the 73-3/4" stiles sit between them — that is the only reading that closes, since 73-3/4" plus 3/4" of rail at each end gives exactly the 75-1/4" side length. The 24" centre rail crosses between the stiles wherever the locker bay meets the drawer cubbies. Join with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, check the frame square on its own, then glue it to the front of the carcass and pin with 1-1/4" finish nails, keeping the outside edges flush with the cabinet sides.

Wrap the base of the cabinet with 1x2 trim and add a matching band at the top. There are no cut-list rows for this trim, so measure the assembled front and sides and cut every piece to your own piece — the surplus in the three 1x2 boards covers it. Miter the two front corners at 45°, cutting the front piece first and the side returns against it, and dry-fit all three before any glue. Pin with 1-1/4" finish nails into the carcass edges.

Cap the top with a final 1x2 band standing slightly proud of the trim below, again cut to fit rather than to a number — the plan gives no dimensions for it. Miter the two front corners at 45° and dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue, adjusting the miters until they close with no gap. On a cabinet 75-1/4" tall this joint sits at eye level and an open miter is the first thing anyone will see. Pin with 1-1/4" finish nails and fill the joints before finishing.

Slide each drawer box into its cubby and confirm it runs without binding before any face goes on. Cut the three drawer faces from the 1x8 — each is your board's full width by 7-1/4", and all three come out of the 2 ft piece with room for the saw kerfs. Hold each face over its drawer with an even reveal all round, tack it from the outside with two 1-1/4" finish nails, then pull the drawer and drive the rest from the inside so no nail head shows on the front. Drill the knob hole through the face and the drawer front together in one pass so it cannot wander.

Lay out the three coat hooks across the locker bay, spacing them evenly and setting them high enough that a hanging coat clears the fixed shelf below. Then secure the cabinet before anything goes in it: at 75-1/4" tall, narrow, and heaviest at the top once the locker fills, it will tip. Find the stud behind it, drill through the back panel and into the stud, and fix it with lag screws or wall anchors. This is not optional trim — it is what keeps the cabinet from coming down on someone.

baseStepTitles: Add sides, top, and bottom shelf
Add hooks and secure to the wall

flags: The existing cut step ended with a garbled lumber-yield sentence — 'one 8 ft Pine, five 8 ft Pine and three 8 ft Pine' — which names no board sizes and is not usable. Replaced with a per-material breakdown verified with packBoards: the 1x2 parts need exactly the five supplied 8 ft boards, the 1x6 parts fit the single 8 ft board, and the 1x4 top trim needs the two 8 ft plus the one 6 ft (50-1/2" + 43-1/2" on one, 50-1/2" + 36-1/2" on the second, the last 50-1/2" on the 6 ft).

The old cut step also listed the 1x3 door frames, 1x4 top trim and base moulding among its materials but never cut them; the rewrite explicitly defers all three to their own steps, which is correct since all are fitted to the finished carcass.

run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box for this plan, so no claim is made about which carcass member is captured. The corner angle on the shelf ends is not dimensioned anywhere in the source — the cut list says only 'ends cut to fit the corner angle'.

ops.body: Break the 3/4" plywood sheet into three 15-3/4"-wide strips first, since every shelf part comes from them. Cut four shelves at 48" with their ends trimmed to the corner angle, one centre shelf at 20", and two dividers at 10-1/2"; the four 48" shelves take two strips and the remaining three parts fit on the third. From the 1x6 cut two sides at 47-1/4", both off the single 8 ft board. From the 1x2s cut four back supports at 47-1/4", two side face-frame pieces at 47-1/4" and three shelf face-frame trim pieces at 46-1/2" — that uses all five 8 ft 1x2s with very little to spare. From the 1/4" plywood cut the centre back panel 24-1/2" x 47-1/4" and two side back panels 13" x 47-1/4". Leave the 1x4 top trim, the base moulding and the 1x3 door frames uncut for now; all three are fitted to the finished carcass in their own steps. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

Hold the two 47-1/4" side panels edge to edge with their ends flush and mark every shelf location across both at once — the shelves are 15-3/4" deep and any drift between the two sides shows up as a tilted shelf. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the sides at each shelf line, then attach with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, working from the bottom shelf up. Check the diagonals across the open front once the second shelf is in. A corner cabinet is on display from two directions at once, so a rack that would hide against a flat wall shows on both faces here.

Fasten the four 47-1/4" 1x2 back supports to the back edges of the shelves first — they give the thin panels something solid to land on. Then attach the three back pieces over them: the 24-1/2"-wide centre panel across the back of the corner, and a 13"-wide panel on each side, all 47-1/4" tall. Nail through with 1-1/4" finish nails into the supports and the shelf edges, keeping the panel edges tight to each other. The shelves are open, so any gap between panels is visible straight through the cabinet.

Cut the 5-1/4" base moulding from the 6 ft stick and wrap it round the base, mitering the outside corners at 45° for a clean return. Cut the front piece first and the two returns against it, and dry-fit all three before any glue — you have one 6 ft stick and no second chance at a miter. Nail it on with 1-1/4" finish nails into the carcass, keeping the bottom edge flat on the floor so the cabinet does not rock on its own trim.

The 3-1/2"-wide 1x4 top trim wraps the cabinet as a mitered ring: two side pieces at 50-1/2", a back-right piece at 50-1/2", a back-left at 43-1/2" and a front at 36-1/2". The cut list marks the last three as having both ends cut 45° off square and NOT parallel, given as long-point measurements — so set the saw, cut one end, then flip the board over to cut the other, or you will get a parallelogram instead of a trapezoid. Dry-fit the whole ring on the cabinet before you commit to any of it. It is not structural, so glue and 1-1/4" finish nails are plenty.

baseStepTitles: Lay out and attach the sides
Build and attach the face frame

flags: ORDER-OF-OPERATIONS DEFECT IN THE SOURCE: step 4 said 'Attach the legs to the top' and step 6 said 'Attach the assembled base to the top' — the same operation twice, in a plan whose own description says the top goes on last. Step 4 has been rewritten as a layout-only step (marking the 1/2" inset on the underside of the top) and step 6 keeps the actual attachment.
No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted; packBoards confirms the eighteen 1x3 parts fit the four supplied 8 ft boards exactly with 1/8" kerf.

The cut list has four 'Second shelf supports' at 4-1/2" but NO boards for the second shelf itself. The rewritten step attaches the supports and says so; a builder cannot complete the second shelf from this plan's data.
The bottom shelf's overall width — 38-1/2", from two 1x3 ends, two 1x12s and two 1x6s all at 24" — is arithmetic on the cut list, but where those six boards sit relative to the legs is not authored.
run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box for this plan, so no claim is made about captured members.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Everything but the top comes off the four 8 ft 1x3s, and every angled cut is 10° — set the saw once per configuration and run all the matching pieces at that setting. Cut eight legs at 29", both ends beveled 10° off square with the ends parallel, long point to short point, so all eight are identical. Cut four bottom supports at 9-3/8" and four second-shelf supports at 4-1/2", all with both ends beveled 10° but with the ends NOT parallel, long point to long point, which makes each a shallow trapezoid. Cut two bottom shelf ends at 24" from the 1x3, two shelf boards at 24" from the 1x12 and two more at 24" from the 1x6 — leave those six square and whole for now, since they get notched later. Those eighteen 1x3 parts pack into exactly the four supplied boards allowing 1/8" per cut, so cut the eight 29" legs first and take the short parts from the offcuts.

ops.body: Each sawhorse is four legs splayed at 10° with supports between them, and you are building two of them, so four leg pairs in all. Mark the inside face of every leg 1-3/4" up from the bottom end — that mark is where the bottom support lands. Fasten a 9-3/8" bottom support between each pair of legs at that mark with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, letting the support's 10° bevels sit flat against the splayed legs rather than forcing them. Build all four pairs the same way, then stand each on a flat floor and confirm it sits without rocking before you go any further; a pair that rocks now will never come right later.

With the notches fitting, drop the shelf boards down over the legs and onto the bottom supports: two 11-1/4"-wide 1x12 boards and two 5-1/2"-wide 1x6 boards, with a 24" 1x3 shelf end capping each outside edge — six boards, 38-1/2" across in total, all 24" long. Push them tight so the edges touch, then fasten down into the supports with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, or glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. Do not worry if a notch overcut slightly into a board; the supports cover it, which is exactly why the notching step told you not to chase perfection there.

ops.title: Lay out the leg positions on the top
ops.body: Flip the 24" x 48" pine project panel face down and mark the leg positions before anything is fastened. Set each sawhorse in 1/2" from the side edge of the panel so the top overhangs its own base evenly rather than sitting flush, and trace round the top of each leg assembly so you can find the position again. The top does not go on yet — the second shelf is far easier to fit while the base is still open on top, so this step is layout only.
materials: Pine project panel, 24 in x 48 in

ops.body: Mark the inside edge of every leg 16-3/4" up from the bottom and fasten a 4-1/2" second-shelf support between each pair of legs at that mark, exactly as you did the bottom supports — the shorter support is what the 10° splay costs you higher up the leg. Use 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Lay a level across both sawhorses and check that all four supports sit at the same height before the glue sets. Note that this plan lists the second-shelf supports but no boards for the second shelf itself, so you will need to size that shelf off the finished frame.

Set the base upside down on the underside of the top, lined up with the marks you traced earlier, and fasten through the top of each leg assembly with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws — drill the pocket holes into the tops of the legs rather than driving anything through the panel face, so nothing shows on the desktop. Turn the desk right side up on a flat floor and check it with a level in both directions. If it rocks, shim under the low foot and re-check rather than forcing a screw down, which will only twist the frame.

Sand every surface, and pay particular attention to the leg ends — the 10° bevels leave a sharp point at each foot that will catch on flooring and on ankles. Ease the shelf edges and the top's edges too. Then prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, giving the desktop an extra coat since it takes all the wear.

baseStepTitles: Notch and fit the bottom shelf
Attach the legs to the top

flags: AMBIGUOUS CUT-LIST ROW: 'Main shelves and back' is one row of four 31" 1x6 pieces with no statement of how many are shelves and how many are back. A single 5-1/2"-wide 1x6 cannot be a back panel for a 34"-tall unit. The rewritten steps say so plainly and tell the builder to allocate the four pieces, rather than inventing an allocation.

run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box, so the assembly prose does not assert which member is captured. However the 33" dowels are 1/2" longer than the 32-1/2" the case measures on the reading where the 31" pieces sit between the two 34" sides, which is stated as the reason to drill through — flagged as derived, not authored.

Dowel hole SPACING and hole height are not specified anywhere in the source. The rewrite gives the technique (clamp both sides and mark together, back the cut, size the bit to the dowel) and tells the builder to lay out four evenly spaced centres, without inventing a dimension.
No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted; packBoards confirms the eight 1x6 parts fit the three supplied 8 ft boards.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Every wooden part is 1x6, so only the lengths change: two sides at 34", four main shelf-and-back pieces at 31", one divider at 11-1/4", and one small shelf at 19-1/4". Those eight pieces pack into exactly the three supplied 8 ft boards allowing 1/8" per saw cut — cut the two 34" sides first, then the four 31" pieces, and take the two short parts from what is left. Cut four dowels 33" long from the 36" stock. Leave the sides' corners square for now: the dowel holes get drilled before the corners come off, because a square board is far easier to clamp and register than one with its corners already cut away.

ops.body: Clamp the two 34" side panels together with their ends flush and mark every hole centre across both boards at once — a dowel needs its two holes at exactly the same height or every roll on it hangs crooked. The plan does not give the hole spacing, so lay out four evenly spaced centres along the 34" length, keeping each at least a full dowel diameter in from the board edges so the hole does not break out. Drill with a 3/4" spade or Forstner bit sized exactly to your dowels; a loose hole lets the dowel and everything hanging on it tip. Drill all the way through and back the work with scrap so the bit does not blow out the far face — the dowels are 33", about 1/2" longer than the case measures across the outside, so each one passes through both sides and stands roughly 1/4" proud. Cut the top and bottom corners off each side with a jigsaw afterwards for a softer profile.

Join the two 34" sides to the top and bottom — two of the four 31" pieces — with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, keeping the outside faces flush. Work on a flat surface, measure both diagonals corner to corner, and adjust until they match before the glue grabs; the holes you have already drilled will only line up for a straight dowel if this case is square. Clamp or weight it while it sets.

The cut list groups four 31" pieces together as 'main shelves and back' without saying how many are which, so decide now: with two used as the top and bottom, you have two 31" boards left to serve as an inner shelf and as the back rail the unit hangs from. Mark the shelf height on both sides at once so it lands level, then glue and nail through the sides into the shelf's ends with 1-1/4" brads. Leave enough clear height below the shelf for the tallest roll of paper you mean to hang. Note that a single 5-1/2"-wide 1x6 is a rail, not a full back panel — this unit has an open back, and its holding power comes from the screws into the studs, not from a sheet.

The 11-1/4" divider stands vertically between two shelves and splits that compartment in two. Set it where you want the split, mark its position on the shelf above and the shelf below so it goes in plumb, and hold it against a square while you glue and nail through both shelves into its ends with 1-1/4" brads. A divider that leans will not take the small shelf squarely in the next step.

The 19-1/4" small shelf spans from one side panel across to the divider, filling the wider of the two bays the divider created. Mark it level on both the side and the divider, then glue and nail through each into the shelf ends with 1-1/4" brads. Because it is nailed into the divider's face rather than through it, drive those brads at a slight angle so they bite rather than slipping along the grain.

Find the studs first and drive the 3" self-tapping screws through the back rail into solid framing — a loaded wrapping station carries real weight for a wall-mounted piece and drywall anchors will not hold it. Drive one screw, level the unit, then drive the rest. Slide the 33" dowels through the drilled holes last; they carry the paper rolls and are meant to pull straight back out when a roll runs out, so leave them dry rather than gluing them in.

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (packBoards): the ten 2x4 frame parts — two at 64-1/2", five at 72", two at 14-1/4", one at 26" — already consume the seven supplied 8 ft 2x4s. The eleven mattress slats at 77" each need a board apiece on top of that, and the optional centre legs in step 11 need more again. The cut list's own note on the slats permits ripped plywood or 1x4s, and the rewrite points at that.

SCREW SIZE NOT IN MATERIALS: the final step called for 2-1/2" screws to fix the slats. This plan lists only 3" and 2" self-tapping screws. Corrected to 2", which is sufficient through the base into a 2x4.
No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted.

FRAME GEOMETRY (arithmetic, not guessed): the 64-1/2" ends run the full width with the 72" joists between them, giving 64-1/2" x 75" overall — confirmed twice, by the 75" side trim and by the 66" end trim (64-1/2" plus 3/4" of side trim at each end). Five joists across that width leave 14-1/4" clear between each pair, which is exactly the centre-support length.

STORAGE TOWER GEOMETRY (arithmetic, not guessed): the 20-1/2" ends run the full height and the 75" top and bottom sit between them, giving a 75" x 19" interior. Confirmed twice: the dividers are 19" and the planked back is 75" long by 19" total width (three 5-1/2" boards plus a 2-1/2" filler). Building it the other way round makes the dividers and the back the wrong size.
The plan fits BOTH a full plywood platform base and eleven slats over it. That is redundant; the rewrite treats the slats as optional over the base, which is how the source's own note reads.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Cut the frame first. From the 2x4s cut two inner frame ends at 64-1/2", five joists at 72", two centre supports at 14-1/4" and the storage support at 26" — be aware that those ten pieces alone consume all seven supplied 8 ft 2x4s, so the eleven 77" mattress slats and any centre legs need stock you have not bought yet; the cut list's own note allows ripped plywood or 1x4s for the slats, which is the cheaper answer. From the two 8 ft 1x12s cut the storage top and bottom at 75" and two storage ends at 20-1/2", one long and one short piece per board with almost nothing left over. From the 1x10 cut two dividers at 19" and three shelves at 24-1/2". From the 8 ft 1x6s cut three planked back boards at 75", and from the 1x3 one back filler at 75". Leave the outer frame trim — two 75" sides and two 66" ends from the 12 ft 1x6s — and the plywood platform base uncut until the frame is standing, since the cut list marks all of it measure-and-cut-to-fit.

ops.body: Lay two of the 72" joists and both 64-1/2" ends out as a rectangle on a flat floor, with the 64-1/2" ends running the full width and the 72" joists between them. That gives a frame 64-1/2" wide by 75" long overall, which the trim confirms twice over: the side trim is 75" and the end trim is 66", which is 64-1/2" plus 3/4" of side trim at each end. Drive 3" self-tapping screws through the 64-1/2" ends into the joist ends, two per corner, predrilling near the ends so the 2x4 does not split. Measure both diagonals corner to corner and shift the frame until they match before anything else goes on.

Add the three remaining 72" joists between the ends, spaced evenly across the 64-1/2" width. With five joists that works out to 15-3/4" on centre, leaving 14-1/4" of clear space between each pair — which is exactly the length of the centre supports, so use one of them as a spacer gauge and the spacing cannot go wrong. Screw through the 64-1/2" ends into each joist with 3" self-tapping screws, predrilling as before. Then fit the two 14-1/4" centre supports between joists at the middle of the span to stop them twisting under load.

Measure the assembled frame rather than trusting the 75" on the drawing, and cut each side trim piece to your actual frame. Run glue along the outside face of the 2x4 frame and pin the 1x6 trim on with 1-1/4" brad nails, holding it so the top edge of the trim sits 1-1/2" below the top of the frame. That 1-1/2" reveal is deliberate — it is the recess the plywood platform base drops into, so it has to be consistent along the whole length. Lay a scrap of 2x4 flat on top of the frame and use its 1-1/2" thickness as a gauge, sliding it along as you nail.

Cut the two end trim pieces to fit — 66" as drawn, which is the frame's 64-1/2" width plus the 3/4" of side trim now standing proud at each end. Glue and brad-nail them on with the same 1-1/2" reveal at the top. These are butt joints, not miters, so cut each end piece last and shave it back until it presses in with the corners tight; a gap here is at knee height and impossible to miss.

Lay the 26" 2x4 storage support flat across the top of the inner frame on whichever side the storage tower will stand, and screw it down into the joists with 3" self-tapping screws. This piece is why the tower does not need legs: everything stored in it loads through this support into the bed's structural frame rather than into a piece of 1x6 finish trim, which would eventually pull off the nails holding it. Position it where the tower's mid-length will fall. That completes the platform.

The storage tower is a long shallow box, and it matters which way round you build it: the two 20-1/2" 1x12 end pieces run the full height and the 75" top and bottom sit between them, so the tower finishes 76-1/2" long and 20-1/2" tall with a 75" by 19" interior. The 19" dividers and the planked back — three 5-1/2" boards plus a 2-1/2" filler, 19" of width in total, all 75" long — both confirm that reading, and building it the other way round leaves every one of those parts the wrong size. Join the corners with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, or 2" screws, and check the diagonals as you go; at 76-1/2" long this box will happily go together as a parallelogram if you let it.

Stand the two 19" dividers inside the box, inset 3/4" from the front edge so their 9-1/4" width leaves a lip at the front of the 11-1/4"-deep box. Mark both the top and the bottom panel for each divider before you fasten anything, so the divider goes in plumb rather than leaning, and hold it against a square while you glue and nail through the top and bottom with 1-1/4" brads. The two dividers split the 75" interior into three bays — put them where you want the bays and mirror the two positions so the tower reads as deliberate rather than accidental.

Finish the platform and the storage tower separately before they ever meet. A full bed with a 76-1/2" tower already attached will not go through a doorway or round a stairwell, and this is worth taking at face value rather than discovering halfway up the stairs. Bring both into the bedroom, stand the tower against the side of the bed, and drive 2" self-tapping screws up through the tower's bottom panel into the bed frame, plus more through the tower into the 26" storage support on the mattress side. That support is what carries the load, so make sure at least two screws land in it.

Cut the 1/2" plywood platform base to fit inside the 1-1/2" reveal you left at the top of the trim, holding it back on the storage side so the tower can sit tight against the frame. Screw it down into the joists with 2" screws and drill a few ventilation holes through it so the mattress can breathe. If you are adding slats over the base, cut them to your own frame and space them about 4" apart, fastening with the same 2" self-tapping screws — this plan lists only 3" and 2" screws, and 2" is the right length through a 1/2" base into a 2x4. Sand every exposed edge and corner before the bedding goes on.

baseStepTitles: Join the platform and storage box
Install feet and add center legs
Add the platform base and slats

flags: No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted; packBoards confirms the 1x12 parts fit the single 10 ft board (111-1/2" of parts in 120") and the seven 1x8 parts fit the two 8 ft boards with roughly an inch to spare.

The 50-1/2" apron length is CONFIRMED by the plan's own numbers: a 53" top with a 1/2" overhang at each end leaves 52" between the outer leg faces, less 3/4" of leg stock at each side, giving 50-1/2". Stated with that proof.

The open mirror span between the two hutch sections is DERIVED, not authored: each section is 11-3/4" plus 3/4" of stock at each side, so 13-1/4" wide, and two of those on a 53" top leave about 26-1/2" between them if the sections sit flush to the ends. The rewrite says to size it to your own mirror.

Step 5 ('Connect the hutch to the base') has an empty materials array and names no fastener size. The rewrite specifies driving up through the tabletop into the hutch verticals but does not invent a screw length, since none is listed for that joint.
run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box for this plan.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Every piece keeps its board's full width, so only the lengths change. From the single 10 ft 1x12 cut the 53" tabletop first, then the two 29-1/4" legs from what is left — that is 111-1/2" of parts out of 120", so there is no room for a mis-cut. From the two 8 ft 1x8s cut the 53" hutch top, four hutch sides and dividers at 20-3/4", and two hutch shelves at 11-3/4"; those seven pieces fill both boards with barely an inch to spare, so lay them out before you start cutting. Leave the 1x4 aprons uncut for now — they are measured off the assembled base two steps from here, and the 50-1/2" in the cut list is only what they should come to. Square every cut and label the pieces.

ops.body: Lay the 53" tabletop face down on a flat surface. Stand a 29-1/4" leg board on each end, set in 1/2" from the end so the top overhangs each leg by that much — with 3/4" leg stock that leaves exactly 50-1/2" of clear space between the inside faces of the legs, which is the apron length in the cut list, so a leg that drifts costs you at the apron. Drill three pocket holes across the top end of each leg, then fasten to the underside of the top with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Check each leg square to the top with a speed square before the second screw goes in, since a pocket screw will happily pull a leg out of square as it seats.

Measure the actual distance between the two legs at the tabletop and cut both aprons to that number — it should come to 50-1/2", and if it does not, your measurement wins over the cut list. Drill pocket holes at each end of each apron and along one long edge. Fasten the first apron up under the tabletop at the front, using the edge holes into the top and the end holes into each leg, with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue; then repeat at the back. The two aprons are the only thing stopping this base racking, so they go on before anything else touches it.

Each hutch section is two 20-3/4" verticals with an 11-3/4" shelf between them, which makes the finished section 13-1/4" wide overall. Build both the same way flat on a table, gluing and driving 1-1/4" brad nails through the verticals into the shelf ends, and set the shelf at whatever height suits what you mean to keep there. Check each section for square before the glue sets and compare the two against each other. The hutch carries no structural load, so nails and glue are genuinely enough here — pocket holes would only add holes to hide.

Stand the two finished sections on edge and lay the 53" hutch top across them. Position them so the top overhangs a little at each end and leaves an open span between the sections for the light-up mirror — with two 13-1/4" sections on a 53" top, that span comes to about 26-1/2" if the sections sit flush to the ends, so measure your own mirror and adjust the positions from there before anything is fastened. Glue and nail down through the top into the end of every vertical with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the back edges of the top and the sections flush.

Set the hutch on the tabletop with its back edge flush with the back of the 11-1/4"-deep top. The hutch is 7-1/4" deep, so that leaves 4" of clear tabletop in front of it to actually work on. Drive screws up through the tabletop from underneath into the bottom end of each hutch vertical — going up from below keeps every fastener off the show surface. Check the hutch stands square to the top from the side before the last screws go in.

Sand everything, easing the front edge of the tabletop and the front edges of the hutch shelves where arms will rest. The pocket holes on the aprons face inward and can simply be left as they are. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, giving the tabletop an extra coat since it takes the most wear and the most spills.

baseStepTitles: Connect the hutch to the base

flags: No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted; packBoards confirms the five 1x12 parts fit two of the three supplied 10 ft boards, and the three 1x4 footer parts fill the single 6 ft board.
Box geometry taken verbatim from run1-box-geometry.mjs: the 32-1/2" sides run the full height and the 40" shelves sit between them, proved by the 41-1/2" x 32-1/2" beadboard back.
The 1-1/2" tabletop overhang at each end is arithmetic — the 44-1/2" tabletop pieces on a 41-1/2" case, centred.
Shelf height for the upper shelf is not authored; the rewrite tells the builder to choose and to mark both sides together.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 10 ft 1x12s cut two sides at 32-1/2", two shelves at 40", and the 44-1/2" front tabletop; those five pieces come off two of the three supplied boards, so you have a spare board if a cut goes wrong. From the 1x3 cut the 40" front top trim. From the single 12 ft 1x2 cut the 40" footer support and the 44-1/2" back tabletop trim. From the 6 ft 1x4 cut the 41-1/2" front footer and two 11-1/2" side footers — that fills the board, so cut the long piece first. Trim the beadboard to 41-1/2" wide by 32-1/2" tall. Hold off mitering the three footer pieces until the case is built and you can measure it, since the miters are cut to the finished carcass.

ops.body: The two 32-1/2" sides run the full height and the two 40" shelves sit between them, which makes the case 41-1/2" wide overall — the beadboard back is cut 41-1/2" by 32-1/2" and only closes on that reading, so do not build it the other way round. Set the bottom shelf flush with the bottom ends of the sides and put the second shelf wherever you want the opening, marking both sides together so it lands level. Fasten through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends with screws and glue, keeping the outside edges flush. Measure both diagonals and adjust before the glue sets.

The 40" 1x3 front top trim spans between the two sides at the top — the same length as the shelves, because it sits in the same place they do. Set its top edge flush with the top ends of the sides and its face flush with their front edges, then glue it and pin through each side into its ends with 1-1/4" brads. Check the diagonals once more here: with the back not yet on, this piece is the only thing holding the top of the case at 40", and whatever shape it sets is the shape the beadboard has to fit.

Lay the case face down and set the 41-1/2" by 32-1/2" beadboard over the back. It is cut to the case's full outside footprint, so its edges land on the side and shelf edges rather than sitting inside them. Because this bookcase has no doors and no closed sides, the back is visible through the whole depth of every shelf, so line the beads up square to the case before a single nail goes in. Tack one corner, check the diagonals, then nail off through the panel into both sides and both shelves with 1-1/4" brads at roughly 6" spacing.

Fasten the 40" 1x2 footer support across the front at the bottom, under the bottom shelf, with its front edge flush — this is what the mitered front footer lands on and it is far easier to fit now than to reach later. Then cut the footer itself: the front piece 41-1/2" and the two side pieces 11-1/2", all mitered at their shortest points, meaning the length given is the short face and the miter runs long from there outward. Route an ogee into the top edge yourself, or use pre-shaped 4" base moulding and skip the router setup entirely. Dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue, then attach with 1-1/4" brads.

Fasten the 44-1/2" 1x2 back tabletop trim across the back edge of the case top first, then lay the 44-1/2" by 11-1/4" front tabletop over the top. Both pieces are 3" longer than the 41-1/2" case, so centre them and you get a 1-1/2" overhang at each end — measure both overhangs before nailing, because an uneven one is obvious from across the room. Glue and nail down with 1-1/4" brads into the sides and the front top trim.

Sand every surface and edge, working up through the grits in the assorted pack and easing the sharp corners on the tabletop and the footer. Get into the beadboard grooves with folded paper by hand — the orbital sander will not reach them and will only round over the bead. Then finish, and do the beadboard back first while you can still reach it easily; it is the visible feature of this piece and the hardest surface to get at once the bookcase is against a wall.

baseStepTitles: Attach the shelves to the sides

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (packBoards): the 1x2 cut-list rows — three at 45", two at 5-3/4", two at 15-3/4", one at 48", four at 45" — need exactly the five supplied 8 ft 1x2s with ZERO slack. Step 6 then calls for 'the remaining 1x2' to be added inside the front for the centre-mount slides, and there is no remaining 1x2. The cut step says to buy a sixth.

CUT-LIST NAMING DEFECT: the face-frame rows call three 45" pieces 'stiles' (45" is the cabinet's inside width) and two 15-3/4" pieces 'end rails' (15-3/4" is the cabinet depth). The names and the numbers disagree. The rewrite says so and tells the builder to dry-lay the frame before drilling, rather than asserting a layout the data does not support.
No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted.

Carcass and drawer geometry taken from run1-box-geometry.mjs: the 45" bottom/shelves sit between the sides, giving 46-1/2" overall (matching the top and the back panel); and the 12-1/2" drawer fronts and backs run full with the 12" sides between them, proved by the 13-1/2" x 12-1/2" drawer bottom.
The original top-edge-trim step called for 3/4" brads into a 3/4" plywood edge, which is too short to hold. Corrected to 1-1/4", which the materials list carries.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Break the two 3/4" plywood sheets into 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips first. From the 15-3/4" strips cut the top at 46-1/2", two sides at 35-1/4" and the bottom at 45"; from the 14-1/4" strips cut two shelves at 45". From the 1x4s cut the 45" back support, two 15-3/4" side trims, the 48" front trim slide support, six drawer sides at 12" and six drawer fronts and backs at 12-1/2" — three of the four supplied 8 ft boards, with one in reserve. From the 1x2s cut three 45" face-frame members, two side rails at 5-3/4", two end rails at 15-3/4", the 48" top edge trim and four 45" shelf trims; that uses all five supplied 1x2s to the inch, leaving nothing for the extra inside-front 1x2 the drawer-slide step calls for, so buy a sixth 8 ft 1x2 now. From the 1x6 cut three drawer faces at 13-3/4". Cut the 1/4" back panel 46-1/2" by 36" and three drawer bottoms 13-1/2" by 12-1/2".
materials: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)

ops.body: The two 35-1/4" sides run the full height and the 45" bottom sits between them, which brings the box to 46-1/2" wide overall — exactly the length of the top and the width of the back panel, which is how you know it is right. Set the bottom at the height where you want the open shelf section to start, glue and screw through the sides into its ends, then cap the whole thing with the 46-1/2" top. While the bottom is still open and easy to reach, drill 3/4" pocket holes facing forward along its underside: that is what the front footer screws into later, and there is no getting a drill in there once the console is closed up. Check both diagonals before anything sets.

Fasten the 45" 1x4 back support between the two sides at the back, level with the top of the drawer bay. It is a 1x4 rather than the 1x2 some older versions of this design show, and that upgrade is deliberate: the centre-mount slides screw into this piece, and a 1x2 does not give them enough to bite into. Attach with pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws. Check it square to both sides, because a slide mounted on a twisted support will bind no matter how well the drawer is built.

Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the console. Be warned that the cut list's naming does not match its numbers: the three pieces it calls stiles are 45", which is the cabinet's inside width, and the pieces it calls end rails are 15-3/4", which is its depth. So lay all seven pieces out dry on the front of the carcass and settle their positions before you drill a single pocket hole. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, check the assembled frame for square on its own, then glue and clamp it to the front inset and flush. Any twist left in the frame becomes twist in the drawer openings.

Wrap the 46-1/2" top with 1x2 trim cut to fit; the cut list gives one 48" piece, which covers the front with enough left over to cut the miters. Miter the front corners at 45° so no end grain shows from the front, cutting the front piece first and the side returns against it. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the plywood edge — 3/4" brads will not reach through the trim into the panel, and a plywood edge holds a nail poorly at the best of times, so keep them back from the very end of each piece to avoid splitting.

Each drawer box is 12-1/2" wide and 13-1/2" front to back: the 12-1/2" fronts and backs run the full width and the 12" sides sit between them, which is what the 13-1/2" by 12-1/2" drawer bottom proves. Build all three the same way — glue and 1-1/4" brads through the fronts and backs into the ends of the sides — then tack the 1/4" plywood bottom on underneath with 3/4" brads and use it to pull each box square before the glue sets. The 13" centre-mount slides need that full 13-1/2" depth, so do not shorten it to make a box fit. Test each finished box in its own opening before you go any further.

Bottom centre-mount slides hang under the drawer, so they need a rail at the front as well as the back support already in place: add a 1x2 across the inside of the front opening for the slides to anchor to, set level with the back support so each slide runs dead flat front to back. Mount the cabinet half of each slide first, then the drawer half, following the hardware's own spacing rather than a number from this plan. Set each drawer so its front sits 3/4" back from the face frame — that recess is what lets the drawer face sit proud of the box and land flush with everything around it.

Cut the three 13-3/4" by 5-1/2" drawer faces from the 1x6 and offer each one up to its drawer with an even 1/8" gap on all four sides. Slip a strip of card or a couple of shims under the bottom edge to hold that gap while you position it, then drive two screws from inside the drawer box out into the back of the face. Pull the drawer right out, add the remaining screws, and slide it back in to check the gaps again before you drill for any hardware.

Fasten the 46-1/2" by 36" back panel on with glue and 1-1/4" brads or staples, nailing into the sides, the top, the bottom and the back support. Tack one corner, confirm the diagonals still match, then nail off at roughly 6" spacing — on a console this wide the back is what stops the box racking. Then fit the remaining front trim: the 48" 1x4 front trim slide support and the two 15-3/4" 1x4 side trims, screwed up into the forward-facing pocket holes you drilled along the bottom in the very first assembly step.

Drop the two 45" shelves into the lower section, level and evenly spaced, and fasten them through the sides or with pocket holes from underneath. They are 14-1/4" deep against a 15-3/4" carcass on purpose: the 1x2 shelf trim goes on their front edges and brings them flush with the front of the case. The cut list gives four 45" trim strips for two shelves, which is enough for a strip on the front and the back edge of each — glue and pin them with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the top edges flush with the shelf surface so nothing catches when you slide something off.

Iron the edge banding onto every exposed plywood edge before you sand — the top, the side edges and the shelf ends will show raw plies otherwise, which is the one thing that makes a plywood piece read as plywood. Trim the banding flush with a sharp blade, then sand the whole console, easing the top's edges by hand. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly; if you are painting, prime the plywood edges first or they will drink the paint unevenly and stay patchy through two coats.

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frame
Attach the back and trim the front

flags: No step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted; packBoards confirms the 2x4 parts fit ten of the twelve supplied 8 ft boards and the 2x6 parts fit all five supplied 8 ft boards exactly (the two 94-1/4" frame members each need a full board).

CONTRADICTION IN THE SOURCE: the cut list names the 42-3/4" pieces 'Pedestal top caps, middle' while the step that installs them is titled 'Attach the remaining bottom piece'. The rewrite names the ambiguity and tells the builder to place all three pedestals identically whichever way they choose, rather than picking a side.
SCREW SIZE NOT IN MATERIALS: the tabletop step called for 2" screws, but this plan lists only 2-1/2" self-tapping and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, plus 2" BRAD NAILS. Corrected to 2-1/2" screws.
Step 4 in the original had no tools key at all; tools have been supplied.

The tabletop frame width of 47-1/4" is arithmetic (36-1/4" cross members between two 5-1/2" long members) and matches the 47-1/4" short frame pieces. The three 16" x 96" panels give a 48" x 96" top over that frame; the materials note's '47-1/2" x 95-3/4" minimum' is 1/4" wider than the frame arithmetic gives, which is within the top's overhang and is noted here rather than resolved.

run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box for this plan; the 70" beam length versus the 94-1/4" frame is stated as a fact (the end pedestals sit inboard) without asserting an inset dimension the plan does not give.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: This is a lot of repeat cuts, so set the saw once per angle and run every matching piece at that setting. From the 2x4s cut twelve outer leg pieces at 22-1/2" and twelve inner leg pieces at 8-1/2", all square; three outer top caps at 47", three middle top caps at 42-3/4" and three base caps at 44", each with both ends at 22-1/2° off square, long point to long point; and three tabletop frame cross members at 36-1/4". That is ten of the twelve supplied 8 ft 2x4s. From the 2x6s cut two long tabletop frame members at 94-1/4" first, since each needs a full 8 ft board, then two beams at 70" and twenty-four cross support pieces at 3-1/4" with one end cut 22-1/2° off square as a longest-point measurement and the other left square — that fills all five 2x6s. From the 1x2s cut six pedestal foot blocks at 3-1/2" and two short tabletop frame pieces at 47-1/4". Check your saw against a square at 22-1/2°, not the more familiar 45°, before you run the twenty-four cross supports.

ops.body: Each pedestal has two upright leg assemblies, so you are building six identical ones. Sandwich the 8-1/2" inner pieces between the 22-1/2" outer pieces so a slot is left down the middle, gluing and pinning with 2" brad nails and keeping every outside edge flush. Before the glue sets on the first one, slide an offcut of 2x6 into the slot and confirm it moves without forcing and without slop — the beams thread through these openings later, and a slot that is tight or loose on even one pedestal throws the whole base out of alignment. Build the remaining five against the first as a pattern rather than against the drawing.

Screw the first layer of outer 2x4 caps to each pedestal with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws: the 47" outer top cap across the top and the 44" base cap across the bottom, both cut with 22-1/2° ends long point to long point so their angled ends overhang the legs symmetrically. Predrill near the ends so the 2x4 does not split. Work on a flat surface and keep the two leg assemblies of each pedestal parallel and the same distance apart top and bottom, so the 2x6 slot still lines up through both. Repeat for all three pedestals and compare them against each other before moving on.

The twenty-four cross supports are 3-1/4" 2x6 pieces with one end cut at 22-1/2° and the other left square, and they stack into the angled fan on each pedestal face — eight per pedestal. Dry-lay a full set of eight on one pedestal before any glue and check that the fan closes evenly; if it does not, your saw is off the 22-1/2° and every remaining piece is wrong, which is exactly why the tool note warns about this angle. Attach with glue and 2" brad nails, working outward from the centre of each face so any accumulated error lands at the edges where it can be trimmed rather than in the middle where it shows.

Add the remaining cap pieces to each pedestal with glue and 2" brad nails — the 42-3/4" pieces, one per pedestal, sitting inboard of the 47" caps already on. Note that the cut list calls these middle TOP caps while the original step called this the remaining BOTTOM piece; the plan contradicts itself, so set all three pedestals side by side and place them the same way whichever you choose, so the base reads as one piece. Finish by fixing the six 1x2 foot blocks, two per pedestal, under the base caps. Stand all three pedestals on a flat floor and confirm they are the same height before any of them meets a beam.

Lay the two 70" 2x6 beams parallel on the floor and mark all three pedestal positions on them, measuring outward from the centre of the beam so the spacing comes out symmetrical. The beams are shorter than the 94-1/4" tabletop frame, so the two end pedestals sit inboard of the table's ends rather than flush with them, and the centre pedestal is what keeps a top this wide from sagging between them. Slide each pedestal onto both beams through the slots you built in the first assembly step, working them along rather than forcing them. Once all three are positioned, fasten through the pedestal legs into the beams with 2" nails or 2-1/2" screws.

Build the tabletop frame first: the two 94-1/4" 2x6 long members with the three 36-1/4" 2x4 cross members between them — one at each end and one at the centre — joined with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. That gives a frame 47-1/4" wide, which is exactly the length of the two 1x2 short pieces that go across the ends. Measure both diagonals; at nearly 8 ft long a small error here opens a visible gap at the end of the top. Then lay your three 16" x 96" project panels across the frame with their edges tight, glue the panel joints, and fasten down with 2-1/2" screws driven up through the frame into the underside of the panels so nothing shows on the surface.

Turn the tabletop upside down on a padded floor and centre the three-pedestal base on it, measuring from the top's edges to the base at several points so the overhang is even all round — the top is 48" by 96" over a frame 47-1/4" by 94-1/4", so you are working to a small margin and an off-centre base shows immediately. Fasten up through the pedestal top caps into the tabletop frame with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws, at least two per pedestal, predrilling so nothing splits. Get help flipping it upright; a top this size is heavy, flexible along its length, and awkward to turn alone.

baseStepTitles: Build the six main leg pieces
Slide the legs onto the beams
Build the tabletop frame and attach the top
Attach the base to the tabletop
